title: Dyeing wool with alder bark
subtitle: Rich tannin brown and warm grey tones from a common riverbank tree
excerpt: Alder bark contains high tannin levels that produce warm brown and grey tones on wool without needing a separate mordant. An iron modifier shifts the colour toward a cool steel-grey. The bark can be gathered from prunings or fallen branches in winter and early spring.
sourceNotes: Alder bark as a dye source documented in Hummel, The Dyeing of Textile Fabrics (1885, public domain, Internet Archive). The high tannin content in alder bark is described as producing brown dyes on unmordanted protein fibres. Iron after-bath modifier producing grey-steel documented in traditional British dye practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A class of plant polyphenols found in bark, galls, and unripe fruit. Tannins bond directly to protein fibres without a metallic mordant, and they also act as a mordant for some synthetic and natural dyes applied over them.
A metallic salt that helps fix dye to fibre. Alder bark's tannin content means an alum mordant is optional; the tannin itself acts as a binding agent.
A substance applied after the dye bath to shift the colour result. Iron sulphate modifier saddens and cools tannin browns toward grey-steel.

content.text: Alder grows alongside streams, rivers, and wet ground across Britain. The bark is easy to gather from prunings after winter coppicing. Its high
content means the bark dyes protein fibres directly without a metallic
, though an alum pre-mordant improves the depth and evenness. The base result is a warm tan to brown; an iron
shifts this to a cool steel-grey.

attrs.title: Mordant safety
attrs.body: Wear long gloves for iron modifier work. Dispose of iron bath via foul drain only. Never pour into a storm drain or watercourse.

items.name: Fresh or dried alder bark (inner bark or outer bark, both work)

content.text: For a more even and deeper result, mordant the wool with 15 g alum at 85°C for 45 minutes before dyeing. This step is optional; unmordanted wool will still take on colour from the tannin alone, but the colour may be slightly paler and less even.

Break or chop the bark into small pieces and place in a clean dye-pot with cold water. Bring to 80°C and simmer for 1 hour. Strain out the bark; the resulting liquor should be a deep amber-brown. Return the dye bath to the pot.

Add the pre-wetted (or mordanted) wool to the strained dye bath. Raise to 80°C over 20 minutes and hold for 45 to 60 minutes, stirring occasionally. Rinse in progressively cooler water and dry in shade.

Dissolve 2 g of iron sulphate in 200 ml of warm water in a separate small pot. Take the dyed, rinsed wool and enter it into the iron bath at 60°C for 15 minutes. The colour shifts from warm brown to a cool grey. Rinse immediately and thoroughly; leaving wool in an iron bath for longer than 20 minutes will weaken the fibre. Dispose of the iron bath via the foul drain only.
The bark dye bath and alum mordant bath can go down the foul drain or be composted. The iron modifier bath must go via the foul drain only; do not use a storm drain, watercourse, or soakaway.

items.problem: The colour is uneven with patchy areas
items.cause: The wool was not pre-wetted or was not moved during the dye bath
items.fix: Always pre-wet the wool for at least 30 minutes before entering the dye bath. Stir gently every 10 minutes during dyeing to allow the dye to reach all surfaces.
